events.title: Military Seizes Power — Coup d'État
events.detail: At 3:00 AM, the Tatmadaw detained State Counsellor Aung San Suu Kyi, President Win Myint, and senior NLD officials. Commander-in-Chief Senior General Min Aung Hlaing declared a year-long state of emergency, citing alleged fraud in the November 2020 elections in which the NLD won a landslide majority. State Broadcasting MRTV went off-air briefly before returning under junta control. The State Administration Council (SAC) was immediately formed as the ruling authority.

events.title: Aung San Suu Kyi & Win Myint Arrested
events.detail: Aung San Suu Kyi was arrested at her Naypyidaw residence and held incommunicado. Charged initially with importing walkie-talkies illegally, further charges including corruption and violation of the Official Secrets Act were added. President Win Myint was simultaneously detained. Suu Kyi was ultimately sentenced to a total of 27 years imprisonment by junta-controlled courts.

events.title: CRPH Formed by Ousted Parliamentarians
events.detail: Ousted NLD lawmakers who evaded arrest formed the Committee Representing Pyidaungsu Hluttaw (CRPH) in Yangon and online. The CRPH declared itself the legitimate legislative body of Myanmar, voided the 2008 military-drafted constitution, and later abolished the laws that protected the military from criminal prosecution. This provided the legal foundation for the National Unity Government.

events.title: Civil Disobedience Movement Launches Nationwide
events.detail: Medical workers, civil servants, teachers, and transport workers launched a Civil Disobedience Movement (CDM), refusing to work under junta authority. At its peak, hundreds of thousands participated. CDM disrupted government functions, hospitals, banks, and government offices across Myanmar. Security forces threatened CDM participants with arrest, but the movement continued throughout 2021 with many participants eventually joining armed resistance.

events.title: Deadliest Day of Protest Crackdowns — 38 Killed
events.detail: March 3, 2021 became the deadliest single day of the anti-coup protests as security forces opened fire on demonstrators in Yangon, Mandalay, Myeik, Monywa, and other cities, killing at least 38 people. The crackdowns used live ammunition, rubber bullets, and tear gas. By April 2021, over 700 civilians had been killed in protest crackdowns. UN Special Envoy Christine Schraner Burgener warned of an 'implosion' if the coup was not reversed.

events.title: National Unity Government (NUG) Declared
events.detail: The National Unity Government (NUG) was announced on April 16, 2021, comprising members of the CRPH, elected NLD parliamentarians, ethnic minority representatives, and civil society leaders. Duwa Lashi La (Kachin leader) was appointed Acting President; Mahn Win Khaing Than (ethnic Karen, former House Speaker) became Prime Minister. The NUG immediately established 17 ministries and began seeking international recognition as Myanmar's legitimate government.

events.title: NUG Declares People's Defence Force (PDF)
events.detail: The NUG officially declared the formation of the People's Defence Force (PDF) as its armed wing on May 5, 2021. The PDF was opened to all citizens willing to defend against military junta violence. Within months, PDF units formed across Sagaing, Magway, Mandalay, Bago, and Kayah regions. Many PDF fighters received rudimentary training from existing ethnic armed organizations (EAOs) before conducting guerrilla attacks on SAC outposts, informants, and government facilities.

events.title: NUG Declares 'People's Defensive War' Against SAC
events.detail: Acting President Duwa Lashi La announced a 'People's Defensive War' against the SAC junta on August 7, 2021, calling on all citizens to 'rise up' against military rule. The declaration aimed to coordinate PDF operations with established ethnic armed organizations (EAOs). This marked the formal transition from protest-era resistance to organized armed conflict. Many EAOs, including the KNLA (KNU), KIA, CNF, and KNPP, agreed to cooperation frameworks with the NUG.

events.title: Karenni/Kayah State Becomes Major Flashpoint
events.detail: Kayah (Karenni) State emerged as one of the earliest and most intense conflict zones. The Karenni Army (KA) and Kayan National Guard, alongside newly formed Karenni People's Defence Forces (KPDF), launched coordinated attacks on SAC outposts. By late 2021, resistance forces had captured significant SAC positions and controlled large portions of rural Kayah State. The SAC responded with heavy airstrikes and artillery bombardment on villages, creating tens of thousands of IDPs.

events.title: Chinland Defence Force Forms in Chin State
events.detail: The Chinland Defence Force (CDF) formed rapidly in Chin State in March 2021, becoming one of the earliest armed resistance groups to emerge after the coup. Chin State, bordering India, saw intense fighting as CDF fighters launched ambushes on SAC patrols using improvised weapons and hunting rifles. The existing Chin National Front (CNF) provided support and coordination. The town of Hakha and surrounding townships became active conflict zones throughout 2021.

events.title: KIA Escalates Operations in Kachin State
events.detail: The Kachin Independence Army (KIA), which had been in conflict with the Tatmadaw since 2011 (ceasefire breakdown), significantly escalated operations in Kachin State following the coup. The KIA attacked multiple SAC outposts, captured supply lines, and coordinated with newly formed PDF units in the region. Myitkyina, Kachin's capital, came under increasing pressure. The KIO (Kachin Independence Organisation) also aligned politically with the NUG.

events.title: Christmas Eve Massacre — 35 Civilians Killed in Hpruso
events.detail: On Christmas Eve, SAC troops killed at least 35 civilians including women and children in Hpruso Township, Kayah State. The victims, predominantly Christians, were burnt alive in their vehicles while fleeing violence. Human Rights Watch and Fortify Rights documented the massacre, attributing it to Tatmadaw soldiers. Charred remains were found at the scene. The massacre drew international condemnation and became a symbol of SAC atrocities against civilians.

events.title: Systematic Village Burning Campaign in Sagaing Region
events.detail: The SAC launched a systematic campaign of burning villages in Sagaing Region, the heartland of PDF resistance. Dozens of villages were burnt to the ground, displacing hundreds of thousands of residents. AAPP, Amnesty International, and local monitoring groups documented over 800 villages burned by 2022. The scorched-earth tactics were designed to deprive PDF fighters of civilian support, food, and shelter. Sagaing became the most severely affected region in Myanmar.

events.title: Airstrike on Let Yet Kone IDP Camp — 60+ Killed
events.detail: SAC jet fighters struck a school in Let Yet Kone village, Sagaing Region, killing at least 60 people including children. The school was reportedly hosting internally displaced persons. The airstrike struck during a public gathering. Myanmar Now and DVB reported it as one of the deadliest single airstrikes in the conflict. The UN condemned the strike as a possible war crime. SAC denied targeting civilians.

events.title: Junta Executes 4 Political Prisoners — First Executions in Decades
events.detail: Myanmar's ruling junta executed four political prisoners: Ko Jimmy (activist and NLD member), Phyo Zeya Thaw (NLD parliamentarian and rapper), Hla Myo Aung, and Aung Thura Zaw. This was the first execution in Myanmar in over three decades. All four had been convicted by military tribunals for terrorism-related charges. The executions drew worldwide condemnation including from ASEAN, EU, US, and UN. Amnesty International called it an 'act of barbarism.'

events.title: Airstrike on Charity Concert in Sagaing Kills 80+
events.detail: SAC jets dropped bombs on a charity concert and fundraiser organized by the Pazi Gyi village committee in Kanbalu Township, Sagaing Region, killing at least 80 people and wounding over 100. The event was attended by civilians and local PDF leaders. Witnesses reported hearing two explosions. The attack caused one of the highest single-incident death tolls in the conflict. Junta denied targeting civilians. Multiple local and international outlets confirmed the mass casualties.

events.title: SAC Extends State of Emergency for Third Time
events.detail: The SAC extended Myanmar's state of emergency for a third consecutive six-month period in August 2022, continuously postponing any return to civilian rule. Under the 2008 constitution, a state of emergency can be declared for six months and extended twice; the SAC repeatedly circumvented this limit. Min Aung Hlaing acknowledged the junta was losing control in much of the country. The extensions allowed the SAC to maintain martial law governance indefinitely.

events.title: Karenni Forces Capture Major SAC Positions Near Loikaw
events.detail: Karenni resistance forces — comprising the Karenni Army (KA), Karenni Nationalities Defence Force (KNDF), and local PDF units — captured multiple SAC military outposts and police stations near Loikaw, Kayah State's capital, by late 2022. Heavy fighting continued around the Kayah-Shan border. The SAC deployed additional troops and air support but failed to regain lost positions. By year-end, resistance forces controlled substantial rural territory across the state.

events.title: SAC Launches Major Ground Operations in Sagaing
events.detail: The SAC launched large-scale ground offensives in Sagaing Region in early 2023, deploying combined infantry, armor, and airpower to clear PDF-controlled territory. Operations targeted Kale, Kalay, and Tamu townships along the Indian border corridor. The SAC temporarily recaptured some towns but faced sustained guerrilla counterattacks. PDF units employed IED attacks on supply convoys and ambushed reinforcement columns, inflicting significant casualties on SAC forces.

events.title: Pazigyi Village Airstrike Kills 170+ at Public Gathering
events.detail: SAC jet fighters and helicopters bombed the village of Pazigyi in Kanbalu Township, Sagaing Region, during an inauguration ceremony for a local PDF-affiliated administration, killing at least 170 people — including women, children, and civilians. UN Special Rapporteur Tom Andrews called it 'an act of pure terror.' It was among the deadliest single attacks of the entire conflict. The SAC claimed it targeted a 'terrorist gathering.' The NUG condemned it as a war crime and called for international accountability.

events.title: KIA Captures Strategic Posts Near Myitkyina
events.detail: The Kachin Independence Army (KIA) launched coordinated offensives in Kachin State in early 2023, capturing multiple SAC bases and outposts on the outskirts of Myitkyina, the state capital. KIA forces also advanced along key highway routes connecting Kachin to Shan State. SAC airstrikes targeted KIA positions and surrounding villages. The KIO coordinated with the NUG and the Three Brotherhood Alliance on strategic planning.

events.title: Karenni Forces Press on Loikaw — Capital Partially Surrounded
events.detail: Karenni resistance forces (KA, KNDF, KPDF) surrounded multiple approaches to Loikaw, Kayah State's capital, by mid-2023, severing key roads and isolating SAC garrisons. While full capture of the city remained elusive, the resistance established checkpoints on main highways. The SAC relied heavily on airlifts to supply Loikaw's garrison. Civilian populations fled, with tens of thousands sheltering in jungle IDP camps.

events.title: SAC Intensifies Airstrikes — Record Civilian Casualties
events.detail: Through 2023, the SAC dramatically increased its use of airpower — jets, helicopters, and drones — to compensate for ground force losses. ACLED documented a record number of air attacks in 2023, with residential areas, markets, churches, and schools targeted across Sagaing, Chin, Kayah, and Kachin states. The junta acquired additional aircraft from Russia and China. UN Special Rapporteur Tom Andrews warned that Myanmar was experiencing 'one of the worst human rights situations in the world.'

events.title: Operation 1027 Launched — Three Brotherhood Alliance Offensive
events.detail: On October 27, 2023, the Three Brotherhood Alliance — comprising the Myanmar National Democratic Alliance Army (MNDAA), Ta'ang National Liberation Army (TNLA), and Arakan Army (AA) — launched a massive coordinated offensive named 'Operation 1027' in northern Shan State. Within days, the alliance captured over 150 SAC outposts, blocked the strategic Lashio–Muse highway, and advanced on multiple towns. The offensive marked the most dramatic shift in the conflict since 2021.

events.title: MNDAA Captures Chin Shwe Haw Border Town
events.detail: Within weeks of Operation 1027's launch, MNDAA forces captured Chin Shwe Haw, a strategic border trading town on the Myanmar-China border. The town had been a key SAC checkpoint and revenue source. Its capture — along with Mong Ko and other border crossings — cut off major SAC supply routes from China. SAC counterattacks with airstrikes failed to dislodge MNDAA forces. The captures shocked the junta, which had relied on Shan State border trade as a revenue source.

events.title: 200+ SAC Outposts Fall in First Month of Op 1027
events.detail: By early November 2023, Three Brotherhood Alliance forces had captured or forced the surrender of over 200 SAC military outposts and 5+ police stations across northern Shan State. Thousands of SAC soldiers surrendered — many reportedly exhausted, underfed, and demoralized. The MNDAA, TNLA, and AA demonstrated unprecedented coordination and firepower. Some SAC commanders reportedly fled without fighting, in contrast to earlier years of bitter resistance.

events.title: Arakan Army Launches Rakhine State Offensive
events.detail: Exploiting the momentum of Operation 1027, the Arakan Army (AA) launched a simultaneous offensive across Rakhine State on November 13, 2023 — dubbed 'Operation 1027 Phase 2.' Within weeks, AA forces captured multiple towns, police stations, and military bases. The AA's offensive opened a second major front far from northern Shan State, stretching SAC resources across the country. By early 2024, AA had captured most of Rakhine's townships.

events.title: MNDAA Captures Laukkai — Kokang Capital Falls
events.detail: On January 5, 2024, MNDAA forces captured Laukkai, the capital of the Kokang Self-Administered Zone (a major Chinese-majority area in northern Shan State). The fall of Laukkai was a major symbolic and strategic blow to the SAC, as it represented the junta's complete loss of control over an entire sub-region. MNDAA released thousands of online scam compound victims — a major issue that had drawn Chinese diplomatic attention. China negotiated a temporary ceasefire shortly after.

events.title: China Brokers SAC–Three Brotherhood Alliance Ceasefire
events.detail: China's Foreign Ministry announced a ceasefire between the SAC and the Three Brotherhood Alliance on January 12, 2024, brokered in Kunming, China. The agreement was partly driven by China's concern over disruption to border trade, scam compound operations, and regional stability. The ceasefire was fragile — the AA continued operations in Rakhine State, which it was not part of. SAC accused the alliance of violating the ceasefire within days. Fighting resumed in northern Shan by mid-2024.

events.title: AA Captures Paletwa Township (Chin State Border)
events.detail: Arakan Army forces captured Paletwa Township in Chin State on January 15, 2024, a strategically important area on the Kaladan River corridor that connects the India-funded Kaladan Multi-Modal Transit Transport Project to Rakhine State. The capture gave the AA control of an economically and strategically significant route. It also demonstrated the AA's ability to extend operations beyond traditional Rakhine State boundaries.

events.title: SAC Announces Mandatory Conscription Law
events.detail: The SAC formally announced activation of the People's Military Service Law on February 10, 2024, requiring mandatory military service for men aged 18–35 and women aged 18–27 for two years. The law — written in 2010 but never previously enforced — was activated as junta troop losses mounted. Penalties for evasion include 3–5 years imprisonment and fines. The announcement triggered a mass exodus of young people from Myanmar, with thousands fleeing to Thailand, India, and China to evade conscription.

events.title: KNLA Briefly Captures Myawaddy Border Town
events.detail: Karen National Liberation Army (KNLA) and PDF forces captured Infantry Battalion 275's headquarters in Myawaddy on April 11, 2024, claiming control of the strategic Thailand-border town. Myawaddy is Myanmar's most important trade corridor with Thailand, handling billions of dollars in annual trade. However, the SAC — assisted by Karen Border Guard Force (BGFK) — counterattacked and KNLA withdrew from the town by April 24. The episode demonstrated resistance capability to threaten major economic hubs.

events.title: Lashio Falls to MNDAA/TNLA — Northern Shan Capital Lost
events.detail: MNDAA and TNLA forces captured Lashio on approximately August 3, 2024, seizing the capital of Northern Shan State — a major SAC military hub and home to the Northeastern Regional Military Command. This was the first time an EAO had captured a regional military command headquarters since the post-coup conflict began. The fall of Lashio represented a catastrophic strategic defeat for the junta, stripping it of control over Myanmar's second-largest state by population.

events.title: AA Captures Maungdaw — Full Bangladesh Border Control
events.detail: The Arakan Army captured Maungdaw Township in northern Rakhine State on December 8, 2024, gaining full control of Myanmar's 270-km border with Bangladesh. Maungdaw was historically the epicenter of the Rohingya crisis (2017 genocide). AA's capture raised concerns about Rohingya refugees' fate and AA's handling of the Muslim minority community. Bangladesh government expressed concern. The capture left the SAC holding only Sittwe (capital) and Kyaukpyu in Rakhine State.

events.title: AA Seizes Ann — Western Regional Military Command Falls
events.detail: On December 20, 2024, the Arakan Army captured Ann Township in central Rakhine State, seizing the headquarters of the SAC's Western Regional Military Command. The loss of a Regional Military Command was unprecedented and signaled near-total collapse of SAC military authority in Rakhine. AA now controlled 14 of 17 Rakhine townships. Only Sittwe (the capital) and Kyaukpyu (a Chinese-linked port city) remained outside AA control, and both were under pressure.

events.title: Operation 1027 Phase 2 Resumed Across Multiple Fronts
events.detail: After a China-brokered pause, the Three Brotherhood Alliance formally resumed Operation 1027 in October 2024, launching renewed offensives in northern Shan State. MNDAA advanced on remaining SAC positions while TNLA pressed on Kyaukme and Hsipaw. Simultaneously, KIA escalated in Kachin, Karenni forces expanded near Loikaw, and PDF continued operations in Sagaing and Magway. The junta faced simultaneous pressure on seven fronts across the country with no meaningful relief.

events.title: 7.7-Magnitude Earthquake Devastates Central Myanmar
events.detail: A 7.7-magnitude earthquake struck near Mandalay at 12:50 PM local time on March 28, 2025, followed 12 minutes later by a 6.7-magnitude aftershock. The earthquake devastated Mandalay, Sagaing, and surrounding regions — already battered by four years of civil war. Death toll estimates ranged from 3,500 (junta official) to over 5,000 (independent media). Historic pagodas, bridges, and hospital infrastructure collapsed. The earthquake struck the same regions that had suffered the most from SAC village burning campaigns.

events.title: SAC Impedes Earthquake Relief — Aid Workers Targeted
events.detail: Following the March 2025 earthquake, SAC forces continued military operations in earthquake-affected zones and impeded humanitarian access. Aid workers and journalists reported difficulty reaching devastated areas due to SAC checkpoints and ongoing fighting. The UN and international NGOs warned that the combination of earthquake damage and conflict severely limited relief delivery. Resistance forces and local organizations conducted independent relief operations. The SAC declared a temporary ceasefire but fighting continued.

events.title: SAC Formally Dissolved — New Security Commission Formed
events.detail: On July 31, 2025, the State Administration Council (SAC) was formally dissolved as the junta's state of emergency expired. Min Aung Hlaing established a new State Security and Peace Commission as the ruling body, retaining all executive authority. Critics noted the dissolution was cosmetic — Min Aung Hlaing remained in command with Soe Win as deputy. The NUG dismissed the restructuring as an attempt to legitimize continued military rule in advance of sham elections.

events.title: Junta-Organized Elections Held — Widely Condemned
events.detail: The SAC/junta organized general elections between December 28, 2025 and January 25, 2026. The NUG, major ethnic parties, ASEAN, the US, EU, and the UN condemned the elections as illegitimate, noting that Aung San Suu Kyi and NLD were banned from participating, elections could not be held in conflict-affected areas (covering most of the country), and the military maintained override powers under the 2008 constitution. Voter turnout was reported as extremely low in areas that held polls.
